title: Green wood vs seasoned wood
subtitle: Choosing the right timber state for your project
excerpt: The single most common source of confusion in wood craft: whether to start from a freshly felled billet or a dried board. This guide explains what each state means, why it matters, how to recognise each by sight and touch, and which projects suit which.
sourceNotes: Cassell's Cyclopaedia of Mechanics vol. II (1883) for timber seasoning methods and moisture effects on workability. Newey & Drage, Practical Carpentry and Joinery (1898) for moisture content guidance on joinery work.

content.text: Every wood-craft project starts with a choice:
green wood
or
seasoned wood

. The choice sets your tools, the feel of the work, and whether the finished piece will hold its shape. The two terms get used loosely in chat. In practice they describe two quite different materials. The working feel, the sourcing, and the finishing rules all differ.
What green wood is

Green wood is timber that has not yet dried. It may be freshly felled, recently harvested from a coppice stool, or stored for a few weeks but not yet meaningfully dried. The cells of the wood still contain water, sometimes close to 100% moisture content by dry weight in fast-growing broadleaves such as willow. The living tree holds water in two forms: free water in the cell cavities and bound water within the cell walls themselves. Both forms are present in green timber.
The working consequence is softness. A well-sharpened sloyd knife pushed along the grain of a green sycamore
billet

produces a clean, thin shaving with very little force. The same knife on a piece of air-dried sycamore board requires noticeably more effort and a sharper edge. For spoon-carving, green wood is almost always the better starting material: the work is faster, the tool marks are cleaner, and the risk of a slipping tool from excessive force is lower.
How to recognise green wood
The end grain darkens within seconds of a fresh cut. If you slice a thin shaving and hold it up to the light, it is slightly translucent and bends without snapping.
The surface of a fresh push cut looks slightly glossy. On seasoned wood the same cut looks dry and slightly chalky.
Chips fly clean and curl away from the blade. On seasoned wood chips tend to break shorter and fall rather than curl.
A fresh cut smells of the living wood: sycamore is faintly sweet, cherry is sharp and resinous, hazel is mild and grassy.
What seasoned wood is

Seasoned wood has been dried, either by leaving it stacked with good airflow (air-drying) or by putting it through a kiln. Air-drying broad-leaved hardwoods takes roughly one year per 25 mm of thickness as a rule of thumb, though species, sawing method, and storage conditions all shift that figure. Kiln-drying takes days to weeks and can achieve a lower final moisture content, but kiln-dried wood is more brittle in some species and less forgiving of subsequent moisture changes.

The target moisture content for indoor furniture is 8 to 12 percent. At this level the wood will not move significantly in a normal indoor environment. Timber for covered outdoor use is typically left at 12 to 15 percent. A moisture meter is the only reliable way to confirm where a piece of timber stands; colour, feel, and weight are rough guides at best.
How to recognise seasoned wood
End grain checks (small radial cracks at the ends of boards and planks) are the clearest sign of air-dried timber. Cracks form as the outside dries faster than the centre.
The colour is duller than green timber, often chalky or grey at the surface. Cross-cutting reveals a lighter, dryer interior.
It is noticeably lighter than an equivalent green piece of the same species: a great deal of the original weight was water.
Which projects need which
Green-wood projects
Spoons, kuksas, mallets, tool handles, willow baskets, hurdles, most riven chair stiles and legs, and garden structures all start green. The softness makes the work practical by hand. A
riven

green blank for a spoon can be rough-shaped with an axe in a few minutes and brought to finished form with a knife in an afternoon. The same project from a kiln-dried blank would take considerably longer and wear both knife and maker more.

The important caveat is movement. Green wood shrinks as it dries, and it does not shrink uniformly: it shrinks more around the growth rings than it does toward the centre of the tree. A spoon with uneven walls: one side thicker than the other, will develop a twist as it dries. Even walls minimise this movement. For simple open shapes (spoons, spatulas, tent pegs, dibbers), the movement is small and usually acceptable. For closed shapes (boxes, drawer parts, anything with a lid that must fit), green wood is the wrong starting material.
Seasoned-wood projects

Boxes, picture frames, small furniture, and any cut joint (dovetails, mortise-and-tenon) need seasoned timber. A green dovetail will lock tight, then open by a millimetre or more as the wood dries. That gap is visible and weakens the joint. Seasoned wood at the right moisture for the room stays still.

Seasoned blanks for carving work are available from specialist suppliers. Dictum, North Star Whittling, and Sculpture House all stock lime, basswood, cherry, and birch blanks sized for first projects. The dimensions are typically printed on the listing; measure the blank before ordering to make sure it has the thickness your project needs before shaping begins.
The special case: turning green and letting it dry

A practised green-woodworker can turn or carve a bowl green, dry it in a steady way, then finish the surfaces. The blank is made slightly oversize with even walls. The bowl warps into an oval as it dries. The maker can keep the oval form, or take it back to the lathe for a final true cut. This is not beginner work. It does explain why some green-wood bowls are oval on purpose.
Sourcing green wood

Good green wood for carving comes from coppice off-cuts, windfall branches, orchard prunings, and the skip or trailer of a tree surgeon. Sycamore, birch, hazel, lime, ash, cherry, and willow are common across the British Isles and western Europe. Sycamore is the standard spoon wood. It is widespread. It grows to carving size fast. It carves cleanly green, dries to a pale food-safe surface, and has no known toxic effect.

Work the wood promptly. A freshly cut billet will begin to dry from the ends within hours in summer. End-coat the cut ends of billets with PVA or old paint to slow the drying and store them in a cool, shaded place if you are not working them immediately. A
blank
stored in a sealed plastic bag in a fridge will stay workably green for several weeks.
A note on food safety

Some species are not food-safe. Yew contains toxic alkaloids in its dust and sap. Pine and spruce are resinous and can flavour food in ways most people find unpleasant. Any reclaimed timber of unknown history (painted or treated wood from building sites, old fence posts) should never be used for food-contact items. The food-safe species for carved treen are sycamore, birch, beech, maple, cherry, lime and basswood, and ash. Walnut carves beautifully and is food-safe after finishing, but it stains dark around iron tools; wipe down after carving with a weak citric acid solution to prevent the staining.

attrs.intro: Things that go wrong when the wood state is mismatched to the project.

items.fix: Carve to even wall thickness throughout, particularly at the transition from bowl to handle. An even 3-4 mm throughout is the target for a dining spoon. Check the wall thickness with calipers as you go.
items.cause: The walls were uneven: one side dried faster and shrank more. The differential movement opened the surface.
items.symptom: A carved spoon develops a crack along its length as it dries

items.fix: For future projects, confirm the moisture content with a meter before cutting joinery. Use timber that has been stored indoors at room temperature for at least a month after purchase.
items.cause: The timber was not fully seasoned when the joint was cut. As the wood dried, it shrank and the joint opened.
items.symptom: A dovetail joint that fit perfectly at assembly now gaps by a millimetre

items.fix: For very dry timber, the axe-and-gouge approach is more effective than a knife alone. Check grain direction: the surface of a push cut should look smooth, not furry; furry means you are cutting uphill against the grain. Reverse direction.
items.cause: Either the wood is too dry for a knife-only approach, or the cut is going against the grain.
items.symptom: The wood is fighting the knife: cuts require heavy force and the surface tears

attrs.heading: Common problems
